speaker: interviewer
text: Gerda Kahan, tape three.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was trying to help people to get out of Poland. One day, we had a party in Bielitz. There were a few Jewish soldiers that came with the Polish Army, which was our fortune, because there was an attack. The Poles tried to-- after the pogrom in Kielce, they attacked the house, the Villa Rapaport. They knew that there were Jews in the house, and they wanted to harm us and kill us. And that was a turning point in my life, because I said to my mother, after spending one year traveling to the border, and I have the connections, this is the time. They were shooting all over the house. There were holes all over the walls. But then, when they saw that there were soldiers and they were shooting back, they ran away. That was the turning point, and I said--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who, wait, the Jewish-- who was shooting back at them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jewish soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were in uniform. They were in Polish-- the Polish Army. So if we decided, I said, that's it, we are leaving. And I went and I got also a visa for myself to go to Czechoslovakia. And we were leaving on the train. Being that everybody knew me on the border, they thought I must have made millions. They took me off the train, because they thought I have so much money. They checked. I had nothing-- a little suitcase with nothing, clothing, nothing. And they-- then they realized that I didn't make money. I didn't get paid for what I did. I didn't want to be paid. I wanted to do something that I owed it to society. And then finally, we arrived in Prague. I continued working in Prague for [NON-ENGLISH]-- not for a long time, until I got the visa to go to Paris. And we arrived in Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't speak French.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: The year? 1945. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't speak French. I didn't know where I'm going. Money, we didn't have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any facilities in Paris to take care of refugees from the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at the time. And I met some young people that knew me from-- I don't know where, Czechoslovakia, Krakow, Krakovets, I don't know. Where you going, Gerda? I said, I don't know. And they told me, there is a small hotel, where a lot of our people are living. Come and maybe you can get a room there. Pink luggage-- I didn't need a porter to carry the luggage. So we went there. And the concierge said, it so happens we have one small room we can give you. OK. And that's the room where we started. There was no kitchen facility. There was nothing. We used the bidet as our stove. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then did you work? What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did work, unofficially, in a lingerie factory. I stretched material. You know, you lay material, one on top of other. And then you put the forms out and you cut it with a machine. I worked there, just in order to have something so that we could buy a baguette.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother worked, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, she couldn't work because, you know, it was illegal. You didn't have-- you were-- we only had a card, a titre de voyage, that you were a traveler. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any intention of staying in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because when we were still in Poland, my mother's youngest brother was in India, in Bombay. And he tried to send us a visa. Every time I went to pick up the visa in Warsaw, they told us it's in Krakow. When I came to Krakow, they told me it's in Warsaw. But after what happened in Bielitz, on that infamous day, I said, the hell with the visa. We are leaving.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I still was thinking that I will one day end up in India, in Bombay, that he will-- the visa will follow us. In the meantime, I met my husband in Paris, who also came alone, and had nobody, and was in a concentration camp, and lost his parents and grandmother. He had a 90-year-old grandmother who went to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And his aunts, and uncles, and sisters, and nephew-- they were all killed in Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we got to know each other. Both didn't have any money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the hotel, the Petit Paradis, by sheer coincidence. Because we didn't like the room. And the lady told us-- the concierge-- that's funny, nobody likes this room. There was a young man that just moved out from this room. So you had it, and you don't like it either. Anyway, it's a long story, but we got to know each other. We used to walk a lot, because we had no money to travel. [LAUGHS] We had no money to go out. So we just went and we had a Perrier, and we were happy. And then we got married in Antwerp in 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why Antwerp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my mother had an uncle, a rabbi there. And we had no relatives. We couldn't even find anybody to be our [GERMAN]. So that was-- we went there. And he give us chuppah Kidusshin. And he-- and David almost fainted under the chuppah, because my uncle was saying, David, I know your father is standing right next to you and your mother is standing right next to you. They are giving you their blessings.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I saw him going down and down. So I started to support his elbow. I saw he is fainting. Because everything was so raw yet. You just couldn't cope with it. After what you have gone through, the fear of all those years-- you know, so many times you think to yourself, is it humanly possible that you can go through such hell and remain sane, and sing, and want to accomplish, and want to achieve? And you want to have a family. And everything becomes important. It doesn't make sense to myself. You understand? I sometimes wonder, how come you get attached again to things that are very unimportant in life? It proved to me that those things are not important. Important was my family. If I would have had my brother, my whole life would have meant something. Picture it, that finally we decided to have children. And when I was-- at that time, I was very rebellious, and so was David. And we were-- and the need to call God to [NON-ENGLISH], that he owes us something. We-- he has to answer why he did-- why he killed the nicest people and he left me alive? I didn't deserve that. But God didn't answer me. So I didn't want to be religious. And I-- I wanted to be rebellious. But when I became pregnant, we sat down one day and said, what now? How do we raise our children? In which direction are we going? And we decided that being that my father died, and my brother died, and my whole family died for the reason that they were Jews-- for no other reason and never committed a crime. They were only born in the wrong place.

speaker: interviewee
text: That the only monument we can give them, and the only think that we can do, is raise our children in a religious way that they should continue. That's the monument. That's what I can give to my father, [SOBBING] that my children will grow up, and be-- at that time I didn't know that I have girls or boys-- whatever. And when my daughter was born, we made the decision-- that's our oldest daughter, Lillian. And then God was very good to us, and we had two more daughters, who are all happily married to wonderful Jewish boys. They have children. My oldest daughter has a girl and a boy. My second daughter has four boys, who are precious, young Jewish boys, who really follow in the footstep of their forefathers. And my youngest daughter has one little girl. I am very, very proud of them. And my whole life is centered around them, to a point that many times I'm saying, my poor kids. Not only am I am survivor, they have to suffer with it. Because love is concentrated to such a point-- what I would have given to my whole family, I concentrated on them. And it's not easy. I am over protective. I have to know everything that they are doing. I am involved in their life as much as they would be little children today. Even my grandchildren, they call me. I just wanted to hear your voice. I just wanted to know that you are OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: They are so-- I never cut them off the umbilical cord. You understand? I only stretched it out to their own individual homes. But there is nothing that I do that isn't concentrated on my children, which is very difficult for them. They feel guilty. I am convinced. They never express that, but I am convinced that it isn't easy. If they do something and I am not there, they feel, oh, mommy is not there and daddy is not there. If I traveled a lot with David when he went into business, I never enjoyed anything unless the children could see it too. I wanted to give them what was denied from me. Because my life could have been a very beautiful story, which ended in such a big tragedy, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I wanted-- like I always say, the two survivors produced a beautiful garden of flowers. [SOBBING] And they are blooming. And this is the only momentum that I gave to my family, and so did David, that my children are continuing in the tradition of- of what their forefathers would have wanted them to be. [CRYING] It's not easy. It's very difficult. How often I can't sleep at night. And today it's a little better, but for years I was haunted by the dogs that were pulling at-- at the legs and bleeding. And I was screaming and sweating cold sweat from fear. It's-- you know, when people came back after the war, everybody said, oh, so good you are alive. I said, no. I was sentenced to go on living. It wasn't easy. And it still is not easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because?

speaker: interviewee
text: The memories are torturing you. It's not that you can forget something like that. It's branding into your body, into your blood. And I am not speaking here for myself. I am speaking for the 6 million that cannot tell you the story. [SOBBING] I am speaking for all my relatives, which is in the hundreds, that cannot tell you anything, because they all were killed. I owe it to them, in their memory, [SOBBING] that I should document that it did happen, that I suffered, and so did everybody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does David also have these hard memories  and nightmares?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes. How often he screams at night and I have to push him to wake him up. I don't tell him anything, just to disturb the dream, so he moves, and then it gets better. But I have suffered so much. I can't begin to tell you-- for years. And I was trying very hard. I have a great sense of humor. But in the morning I try-- but after a night of not sleeping, and lying in cold sweat from fear-- and you know, the biggest story behind all of this, I'm always trying to save my mother. I'm always running to save her. It's like [?haunting?] me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That I couldn't save my father or my brother-- that's unfortunate. And blessed is their memory, because they were very special people. [SOBBING] Anybody that knows them, or knew them, will tell you. My brother has one friend. He always tells me that he mourns him the same as he mourns his family. Because he was a very special human being. He was gorgeous, and talented. And my whole life was always centered, my brother, my brother. Now, unfortunately, it didn't last long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you or David help each other with these memories, with these stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, I think that I am more fortunate than David, because I can verbalize it. David is like in a denial. David cannot accept the fact that maybe that he was a little boy and he couldn't do anything about it, and that his father was the head of the community. His father was a very active member of the Czech community. He established a kosher kitchen in in-- in Czechoslovakia, in the Army. He was a very prominent man.

speaker: interviewer
text: His-- he used to go to the Yeshiva, to listen to the children, if they learn. He was [NON-ENGLISH], you know, the head of the community. And anybody that knew him-- he was somebody very special. And I think he cannot accept that, that his father, who was somebody so special-- his mother used-- you know, in Czechoslovakia there was a community that there were a lot of poor people, right? His mother cooked every Thursday for hundred people, specially that they should have a hot meal-- Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: --before the war-- right. And when, God, forbid, she used to mark down how many people came, if there were some missing, she was terribly worried. What happened that they didn't come? You know, he was raised in a very rich home. And I think it's difficult for him to accept he cannot unite [POINTS] the past with the future.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about, do you tell the children what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they know?

speaker: interviewee
text: To a certain degree. Because it's very difficult that they should understand it. I think the more they read and the more they hear, the better it is for them to understand it. And I tell you honestly, many times, I myself think, is it possible? Did I really go through such hell? Was I really beaten like a dog? Was I really that hungry for three years and cold and-- and destroyed? And the fear, 24 hours of fear-- you cannot-- no picture, no film, no story, no book can portray the actual fear that one lived, that you were sitting all evening long killing lice. It was like music. Because in your-- you couldn't live like this. The clothes-- it was eating you up, and you were just ticking them on your nails. And people were counting, like a joke. Ah, that was movie, that was entertainment. You cannot show that in a movie. It is below any human dignity to live under such circumstances-- no water, no bathroom, the snow was-- Poland is very cold. The barracks were not built with insulation. So the wind blew in the snow through, between. There was no heat. There-- it just-- and you live. And now you complain, my bones hurt me. No wonder that they hurt. I'm surprised that anyone can walk. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because it wasn't-- you didn't have clothes. You didn't have food. You have only torture.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many times did I see that when they gave out the soup, they took the ladle and hit somebody over the head? The blood was running down. I mean, can you forget that? Can you forget the people that you knew today, and an hour later you knew that they were killed, and you just heard the shooting? Or you watched them get undressed. Can you ever forget that? Can you tell the world that they were silent? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That somebody has to speak up? That it isn't a joke, that it's not only a story? Because today, when you look back at the Inquisition, you're saying, that was such a long time. But that long time-- those people are still alive here that have suffered more than anybody can understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not only that we suffered-- we suffered also for those that-- that didn't come back. Because they were not killed on the first day of the war. They suffered for years, until they were destroyed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had the same hopes, like any other child in the world. They had the same ambition. My brother was a brilliant boy, brilliant! Show me another child that comes from a foreign country and has in 24 subjects, excellent, without knowing the language. He had to learn it in three months. He was a designer. I mean, show me. He had the same hopes, like everybody else. He wanted to live. And he was gorgeous on top of it. It's not for me to judge. And I tell you from the beginning, God owes me an answer. But maybe he owed me that answer and he gave it to me, [SOBBING] that I have seven grandchildren, who thanks God are all healthy, and good looking, and-- and behave like human beings, which is so hard after all that we went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to-- I just want to pick up the story a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: After Paris, can you just tell us how you got to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I got a visa, because I had the German quota. And I came here. And my mother couldn't come, because she had to wait. And she stayed three and a half years longer in Europe. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And David came too?

speaker: interviewee
text: David came also. As a matter of fact, we came both as single people on the same boat. It was a Russian boat. And we arrived in New York on the 23rd of November in 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in order to make it a little light, I will tell you that David spoke very good English. I didn't speak a word of English. And David had studied medicine once upon a time, which also all ended in a dream. And I asked him whether he could get me an orange juice, all right? And he went over to the man at the pier. And he said, could I have an orange juice? He said, sure. How much is it? A dime. And David said, excuse me, what is a dime? And the man says, wise guy. Two nickels! And he said, what is a nickel? And he said, you mean to say you speak so well English? There is a line here. Don't you see what you are doing? He said, no, I don't know. He said, 10 cents. Because it's-- that's slang. And it-- the difficulties that you went through, even after the war, not knowing the language, not having a place where to stay. I prayed that this boat that I came on should go forever. Because I had a roof over my head. I was petrified of coming to New York, not knowing the language, not knowing anything, having no money. Right? Where does one start? How did you get to New York? I mean, did you have somebody to come to in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. So when we docked in the pier on 40-- on the West Side, I was standing there. And I saw the skyscrapers and all of the cars. I said, oh my God, this is impossible. I've gone through such hell, and now I'm coming here. I don't know anything and anybody?

speaker: interviewer
text: To a point, I was in such a need of belonging. I was in such a need that I am not only a number or a Jewish whore, as I was called for so many years, right? And that one day-- I didn't speak Yiddish, and I didn't speak English. And one day I went to a greengrocer on the West Side. And as I approached him, the man says to me in Yiddish, what do you want? But [YIDDISH], you know, like, [YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And I turned to David. I said, David, he knows me. He calls me [GERMAN]. I was so excited. I needed to have roots.

speaker: interviewer
text: [GERMAN] is the German for-- familiar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. That I-- that I-- somebody knows me, that I'm not only a number, that I had a family, and I had parents, and that they loved me just as much as anybody else. That I didn't grow up like that dog that was beaten. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's not easy to call this life after what you have gone through. It's a real hardship. Because you had to-- it's like a puzzle, and you're putting the pieces together. And there's always one piece missing, and you don't know where to put it. That's your past, that you're looking for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened? You went through Ellis Island when you came?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't go through Ellis Island. I went to Ellis Island for a hearing afterwards. Because [LAUGHS] they thought I am German, and I had to go-- I have a file there like a criminal that I did not belong to the Hitler Youth. And I had to swear. And I had to report to them that I didn't belong to Hitler Youth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did-- just because you were German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So I said to the officer there in charge-- I said, it's very funny. And for that, I had to spend three and a half years in a concentration camp, that you should ask me today whether I belonged to the Hitler Youth. I would have loved it, right? But you're talking to the wrong party. Go and look for all the Germans that the United States had let in. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and how did you-- where did you settle in New York when you came?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, we lived on the West Side, in a furnished room. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get any help any of the organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we got from the Association of New Americans. They helped us until-- David speaks seven languages. So he got a job. It wasn't allowed yet at that time to work, because until the DP Act went through that you are a displaced person-- which I wasn't accepted under, because I could go back to Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had to go out to Canada and re-immigrate, [LAUGHS] because I had an American child. Otherwise, they told me I can go back to Germany. [SIGHS] So David worked as a translator interpreter for the United Association for New Americans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because most of the people did not speak any other language but their native one. And he spoke, so he helped. And that's how we started.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and you lived in the West Side?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we lived on the West Side. And then we moved to Washington Heights. We got a small apartment over there. But it didn't matter, because you felt free-- free only in the sense that you could walk out. But I wasn't free from the inside. I felt tortured. I was afraid of my own shadow. I walked on the street. I only looked behind me, if there was anybody behind me. It took years to shake that off, years. I still have fear. I have tremendous fear of thunderstorms, because it reminds me of the bombing-- not so much the lightning as the thunder, because the bombing and the-- tremendous-- that when my children were little, I couldn't tell them that I am afraid of a thunderstorm. I was ashamed. I used to hide in the closet or in a hallway where there was no window-- I shouldn't hear it so well. I mean, I-- they have done a marvelous job of destroying us. We are going on. He spoke. We are going on, but it's not a full life that one lives. The memories are tremendous.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was wonderful.
